---
title: "Work with Models"
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

This chapter follows [Chapter 6 of @Healy2026](https://socviz.co/06-modeling.html), translating the main model-based graphics to Python, Polars, `statsmodels`, and `plotnine_polars`.
The goal is not to teach statistical modeling, but to show how model objects can be turned into tidy tables that we can graph.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import polars as pl
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

from mizani.formatters import currency_format
from plotnine_polars import aes, position_dodge
from socviz_pl import load_data
```

```{python}
gapminder = pl.read_csv(
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jennybc/gapminder/main/inst/extdata/gapminder.tsv",
    separator="\t"
)
gapminder_pd = gapminder.to_pandas()

gss_sm = load_data("gss_sm")
gss_sm_pd = gss_sm.to_pandas()
```

## Show Several Fits at Once

Plotnine can fit several smoothers directly inside a plot, much as ggplot does.
For the robust regression smoother, plotnine currently warns that confidence intervals are not implemented, so these examples suppress the interval for that layer.

```{python}
p = (
    gapminder
    .with_columns(log_gdp=pl.col("gdpPercap").log())
    .ggplot(aes(x="log_gdp", y="lifeExp"))
)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-06-ols-rlm
#| fig-cap: "Comparing ordinary least squares and robust regression smoothers."
(
    p
    .geom_point(alpha=0.1)
    .geom_smooth(color="tomato", fill="tomato", method="rlm", se=False)
    .geom_smooth(color="steelblue", fill="steelblue", method="lm")
)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-06-spline
#| fig-cap: "A cubic spline smoother."
(
    p
    .geom_point(alpha=0.1)
    .geom_smooth(
        color="tomato",
        method="lm",
        formula="y ~ bs(x, df=3)",
        se=False,
        size=1.2
    )
)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-06-quantile
#| fig-cap: "Quantile regression lines."
(
    p
    .geom_point(alpha=0.1)
    .geom_quantile(
        color="tomato",
        size=1.2,
        quantiles=[0.20, 0.50, 0.85]
    )
)
```

When separate smoother layers map `color` and `fill` to literal labels, plotnine can build a legend for the model types.
As in R, this is a useful trick: the labels are not variables in the data, but one-value aesthetic mappings created for each layer.

```{python}
model_colors = ["#E41A1C", "#377EB8", "#4DAF4A"]
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-06-smoothers-legend
#| fig-cap: "Fitting several smoothers with a legend."
(
    p
    .geom_point(alpha=0.2)
    .geom_smooth(
        aes(color='"OLS"', fill='"OLS"'),
        method="lm"
    )
    .geom_smooth(
        aes(color='"Cubic Spline"', fill='"Cubic Spline"'),
        method="lm",
        formula="y ~ bs(x, df=3)"
    )
    .geom_smooth(
        aes(color='"LOESS"', fill='"LOESS"'),
        method="loess"
    )
    .scale_color_manual(name="Models", values=model_colors)
    .scale_fill_manual(name="Models", values=model_colors)
    .add_theme(legend_position="top")
)
```

## Look Inside Model Objects

Statsmodels uses formulas via `patsy`, so model fitting happens most naturally against a pandas data frame.
The companion keeps Polars as the main data-frame library, but converts to pandas at the modeling boundary.

```{python}
out = smf.ols(
    "lifeExp ~ gdpPercap + pop + C(continent)",
    data=gapminder_pd
).fit()
```

```{python}
print(out.summary())
```

The printed summary is convenient for reading, but not convenient for plotting.
The following small helpers provide the pieces of `broom::tidy()`, `augment()`, and `glance()` that this chapter needs.

```{python}
def tidy_result(result, conf_int=False):
    out = pd.DataFrame({
        "term": result.params.index,
        "estimate": result.params.to_numpy(),
        "std_error": result.bse.to_numpy(),
        "statistic": result.tvalues.to_numpy(),
        "p_value": result.pvalues.to_numpy(),
    })
    if conf_int:
        conf = result.conf_int()
        out["conf_low"] = conf.iloc[:, 0].to_numpy()
        out["conf_high"] = conf.iloc[:, 1].to_numpy()
    return pl.from_pandas(out)


def augment_ols(result, data):
    pred = result.get_prediction(data).summary_frame()
    out = data.copy()
    out["fitted"] = result.fittedvalues
    out["se_fit"] = pred["mean_se"].to_numpy()
    out["resid"] = result.resid
    out["hat"] = result.get_influence().hat_matrix_diag
    out["cooksd"] = result.get_influence().cooks_distance[0]
    return pl.from_pandas(out)


def glance_ols(result):
    return pl.DataFrame({
        "r_squared": [result.rsquared],
        "adj_r_squared": [result.rsquared_adj],
        "sigma": [np.sqrt(result.mse_resid)],
        "statistic": [result.fvalue],
        "p_value": [result.f_pvalue],
        "df": [result.df_model + 1],
        "log_lik": [result.llf],
        "aic": [result.aic],
        "bic": [result.bic],
        "deviance": [np.sum(result.resid ** 2)],
        "df_resid": [result.df_resid],
    })
```

```{python}
tidy_result(out).with_columns(pl.selectors.numeric().round(2))
```

## Generate Predictions to Graph

R's `expand.grid()` creates all combinations of supplied values.
Here Polars can do the same thing by building the columns explicitly and cross-joining them.

```{python}
gdp_grid = pl.DataFrame({
    "gdpPercap": np.linspace(
        gapminder["gdpPercap"].min(),
        gapminder["gdpPercap"].max(),
        100
    )
})
pop_grid = pl.DataFrame({"pop": [gapminder["pop"].median()]})
continent_grid = pl.DataFrame({
    "continent": ["Africa", "Americas", "Asia", "Europe", "Oceania"]
})

pred_df = (
    gdp_grid
    .join(pop_grid, how="cross")
    .join(continent_grid, how="cross")
)
pred_df.shape
```

```{python}
pred_out = out.get_prediction(pred_df.to_pandas()).summary_frame()

pred_df = pl.concat(
    [
        pred_df,
        pl.from_pandas(
            pred_out[["mean", "obs_ci_lower", "obs_ci_upper"]]
            .rename(columns={
                "mean": "fit",
                "obs_ci_lower": "lwr",
                "obs_ci_upper": "upr",
            })
        )
    ],
    how="horizontal"
)
pred_df.head()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-06-ols-predictions
#| fig-cap: "OLS predictions for Europe and Africa."
pred_eu_af = pred_df.filter(pl.col("continent").is_in(["Europe", "Africa"]))
gap_eu_af = gapminder.filter(pl.col("continent").is_in(["Europe", "Africa"]))

(
    pred_eu_af
    .ggplot(aes(
        x="gdpPercap",
        y="fit",
        ymin="lwr",
        ymax="upr",
        color="continent",
        fill="continent",
        group="continent"
    ))
    .geom_point(
        data=gap_eu_af,
        mapping=aes(x="gdpPercap", y="lifeExp", color="continent"),
        alpha=0.5,
        inherit_aes=False
    )
    .geom_line()
    .geom_ribbon(alpha=0.2, color=None)
    .scale_x_log10(labels=currency_format(precision=0, big_mark=","))
)
```

## Tidy Model Objects

The coefficient table can be plotted once it is turned into a data frame.
Categorical terms from statsmodels include `C(continent)[T.<level>]`; for display we strip that formula syntax away.

```{python}
out_conf = (
    tidy_result(out, conf_int=True)
    .filter(pl.col("term") != "Intercept")
    .with_columns(
        nicelabs=(
            pl.col("term")
            .str.replace("C\\(continent\\)\\[T\\.", "")
            .str.replace("\\]", "")
        )
    )
)

term_order = (
    out_conf
    .sort("estimate")
    .get_column("nicelabs")
    .to_list()
)
out_conf = out_conf.with_columns(pl.col("nicelabs").cast(pl.Enum(term_order)))

out_conf
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-06-coef-basic
#| fig-cap: "A basic plot of OLS estimates."
(
    out_conf
    .ggplot(aes(x="nicelabs", y="estimate"))
    .geom_point()
    .coord_flip()
)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-06-coef-intervals
#| fig-cap: "OLS estimates and confidence intervals."
(
    out_conf
    .ggplot(aes(
        x="nicelabs",
        y="estimate",
        ymin="conf_low",
        ymax="conf_high"
    ))
    .geom_pointrange()
    .coord_flip()
    .labs(x=None, y="OLS Estimate")
)
```

Observation-level statistics are also useful.
The helper here omits broom's leading dots in column names, because plain Python expressions and plotnine mappings are easier to read without them.

```{python}
out_aug = augment_ols(out, gapminder_pd)
out_aug.select("country", "continent", "year", "lifeExp", "fitted", "resid", "hat", "cooksd").head()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-06-residuals-fitted
#| fig-cap: "Residuals versus fitted values."
(
    out_aug
    .ggplot(aes(x="fitted", y="resid"))
    .geom_point(alpha=0.35)
)
```

```{python}
glance_ols(out).with_columns(pl.selectors.numeric().round(2))
```

Statsmodels also has duration-modeling tools, including Cox proportional hazards models through `PHReg`.
The survival-analysis aside from the R chapter is omitted here because it is not central to this companion's first-pass translation.

## Grouped Analysis

The R chapter uses nested list columns and `purrr::map()` to fit one model per continent-year group.
In Python, an explicit loop is clearer for this first pass.

```{python}
def fit_life_exp_group(df):
    fit_df = df.assign(log_gdp=np.log(df["gdpPercap"]))
    result = smf.ols("lifeExp ~ log_gdp", data=fit_df).fit()
    return tidy_result(result).to_pandas()


group_results = []
for (continent, year), df in gapminder_pd.groupby(["continent", "year"], sort=True):
    if continent == "Oceania":
        continue
    tidied = fit_life_exp_group(df)
    tidied["continent"] = continent
    tidied["year"] = year
    group_results.append(tidied)

out_tidy = (
    pl.from_pandas(pd.concat(group_results, ignore_index=True))
    .filter(pl.col("term") == "log_gdp")
    .with_columns(
        ymin=pl.col("estimate") - 2 * pl.col("std_error"),
        ymax=pl.col("estimate") + 2 * pl.col("std_error"),
    )
)

out_tidy.sample(5, seed=123)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-06-grouped-ols
#| fig-cap: "Yearly estimates of the association between GDP and life expectancy, pooled by continent."
(
    out_tidy
    .ggplot(aes(
        x="year",
        y="estimate",
        ymin="ymin",
        ymax="ymax",
        group="continent",
        color="continent"
    ))
    .geom_pointrange(position=position_dodge(width=1))
    .scale_x_continuous(breaks=gapminder["year"].unique().sort().to_list())
    .add_theme(legend_position="top")
    .labs(x="Year", y="Estimate", color="Continent")
)
```

## Plot Marginal Effects

Statsmodels can estimate logistic regression models and calculate average marginal effects.
The model below mirrors the R example by predicting the binary `obama` variable from political views, sex, race, and a sex-by-race interaction.

```{python}
bo_df = (
    gss_sm_pd
    .assign(polviews_m=gss_sm_pd["polviews"])
    [["obama", "polviews_m", "sex", "race"]]
    .dropna()
)

out_bo = smf.logit(
    'obama ~ C(polviews_m, Treatment(reference="Moderate")) + '
    'C(sex, Treatment(reference="Male")) * '
    'C(race, Treatment(reference="White"))',
    data=bo_df
).fit(disp=False)
```

```{python}
print(out_bo.summary())
```

```{python}
bo_m = out_bo.get_margeff(at="overall", dummy=True)
bo_gg = (
    pl.from_pandas(
        bo_m.summary_frame()
        .reset_index(names="factor")
        .rename(columns={
            "dy/dx": "AME",
            "Std. Err.": "SE",
            "Pr(>|z|)": "p",
            "Conf. Int. Low": "lower",
            "Cont. Int. Hi.": "upper",
        })
    )
    .with_columns(
        factor=(
            pl.col("factor")
            .str.replace('C\\(polviews_m, Treatment\\(reference="Moderate"\\)\\)\\[T\\.', "")
            .str.replace('C\\(sex, Treatment\\(reference="Male"\\)\\)\\[T\\.', "")
            .str.replace('C\\(race, Treatment\\(reference="White"\\)\\)\\[T\\.', "Race: ")
            .str.replace_all("\\]", "")
            .str.replace_all(":Race: ", " x Race: ")
        )
    )
)

ame_order = bo_gg.sort("AME").get_column("factor").to_list()
bo_gg = bo_gg.with_columns(pl.col("factor").cast(pl.Enum(ame_order)))

bo_gg.select("factor", "AME", "lower", "upper")
```

Statsmodels reports marginal effects for the model matrix columns, including the interaction columns.
This is not identical to the `margins` output in the R chapter, but it serves the same purpose here: turning a fitted logistic model into a tidy table of effect estimates and intervals.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch-06-average-marginal-effects
#| fig-cap: "Average marginal effects from a logistic regression."
(
    bo_gg
    .ggplot(aes(x="factor", y="AME", ymin="lower", ymax="upper"))
    .geom_hline(yintercept=0, color="#CCCCCC")
    .geom_pointrange()
    .coord_flip()
    .labs(x=None, y="Average Marginal Effect")
)
```

The R chapter also uses `margins::cplot()` for conditional effects.
There is no close statsmodels-native analogue that keeps the example as compact, so this first pass omits that plot.

## Complex Surveys

The original chapter closes with examples using the R `survey` and `srvyr` packages.
Python does not currently offer a close equivalent within this companion's dependency stack, and a manual approximation would risk teaching the wrong thing about complex survey designs.
For that reason, the complex-survey section is omitted here rather than translated loosely.

## Where to Go Next

The main translation lesson is that model-based graphics usually require two steps in Python: fit the model at the pandas/statsmodels boundary, then turn the result into a tidy Polars table for plotting.
The hard part is still deciding what quantities should be shown.
